metric_value: <$100 million
notes: Aberdeen estimates market for store-and-forward messaging APIs and tools at less than $100M in 1995, comparable to X.400 email products of 1980s

metric_value: ~$1 billion
notes: Billion-dollar groupware market dominated by Lotus Notes; uses store-and-forward and Workflow Manager technology

metric_value: ~$1 billion
notes: Billion-dollar e-mail market; store-and-forward messaging is the major communications mechanism for email products

metric_value: $100-200 million
notes: Second-generation CADEs achieving $100-200M in revenue; includes publish-and-subscribe APIs in many cases; splitting messaging revenue out is problematic

metric_value: <$10 million
notes: Market for distributed objects below $10M; technology is hidden (encapsulated) within objects in current products

metric_value: hundred-million range
notes: DCE constitutes a small hundred-million range submarket of the overall NOS market; provides location independence but not direct messaging APIs

metric_value: market leader; sophisticated; highest flexibility, scalability, programmability among evaluated products
notes: Aberdeen finds MQSeries has multithreading, traces for testing/monitoring, assured once-and-only-once delivery, and single-point-of-control systems management on some platforms

metric_name: MQSeries platform and protocol support breadth
metric_value: Unix, AS/400, MVS/ESA, Tandem; Windows/DOS client; Windows 95 and NT upcoming; TCP/IP, SNA, NetBIOS, DECnet simultaneous
notes: Broad multiplatform coverage; simultaneous multi-protocol support is a differentiating capability

metric_value: will not provide Commercial Messaging capabilities in near future
notes: Aberdeen finds major distributed-object players including CORBA consortium will not provide Commercial Messaging capabilities in near future

metric_value: will not provide Commercial Messaging capabilities in near future
notes: Consistent with CORBA finding; Microsoft not delivering Commercial Messaging through distributed-object platform as of 1995

metric_value: focused on e-mail market; transaction support planned within 2 years; not a full Commercial Messaging product
notes: HP OpenMail offers APIs for multiple standard e-mail APIs across enterprise products; HP indicated transaction support on 2-year roadmap

metric_value: store-and-forward architecture present; full developer APIs not yet provided
notes: RDBMS suppliers with store-and-forward architecture have not yet fully provided APIs/tools for developers to use Commercial Messaging

metric_value: well integrated into toolset; pub/sub present; messaging not easily portable to applications outside toolset
notes: Second-generation CADE with store-and-forward; messaging does not necessarily transfer to applications written outside these toolsets

metric_value: outstanding example; repartitions and rebundles components online transparently with Commercial Messaging
notes: Aberdeen cites Forte as outstanding example of application partitioning via Commercial Messaging; repository/directory and dev tools fully integrated

metric_value: 6-7x in 18 months
notes: Aberdeen finds data-warehouse databases growing at 6-7 times in 18 months in many cases; major driver of scalability demand for Commercial Messaging

metric_name: IS adoption level of Commercial Messaging
metric_value: underutilized; IS not yet taking effective advantage
notes: Aberdeen research shows IS organizations are not taking effective advantage of Commercial Messaging despite strategic importance and rapid product spread

metric_value: e-mail is by far most frequent use; technology invisible to users
notes: E-mail is the dominant visible form of store-and-forward; developers not exploiting the underlying technology directly in enterprise applications

metric_name: Legacy application retrofit risk from delayed adoption
metric_value: growing mass of networking-limited legacy applications; costly retrofit or early rearchitecting required
notes: Delay adds to legacy debt; new client-server applications without Commercial Messaging may fail to scale in distributed enterprise and require early costly rearchitecting

metric_value: no one-stop shop; market not yet developed for full bundled solution from single supplier
notes: No supplier presently provides Queue Manager plus Workflow Manager plus dev toolset plus admin tools plus service by itself; seek one-stop storefront strategy

metric_value: VB and PowerBuilder dominant over second-generation CADEs at departmental level
notes: Many Fortune-1000 departmental projects use VB or PowerBuilder rather than second-gen CADEs; limits reach of CADE-embedded messaging

metric_value: openness; broad scope of applicability; customizability; ability to evolve as market matures
notes: First of three key yardsticks Aberdeen identifies for long-term Commercial Messaging product value; test via VB/PowerBuilder interop and data replication scope

metric_value: multithreading; multiple queues; effective caching and memory management; pointer-passing vs. copying; administrative reconfiguration
notes: Second of three key yardsticks; driven by 6-7x data warehouse growth; multithreading and parallel hardware support are critical performance features

metric_value: APIs for wide range of tasks; coding and testing support; design tools for Queue Manager placement on enterprise internetwork
notes: Third of three key yardsticks; developer productivity and quality are the primary measures

metric_value: potentially on verge of major increase in strategic importance to enterprise IS
notes: Aberdeen predicts major increase driven by laptop support, data warehousing, application servers, and programmer-productive client-server development

metric_value: will proliferate for specific user needs; more suppliers at all levels will enter market
notes: Aberdeen predicts Workflow Managers and Agents will proliferate; Commercial Messaging dev tools will integrate more effectively with CADEs and RDBMSs

metric_value: users will eventually see Commercial Messaging as applicable to most or all legacy and new applications
notes: Aberdeen predicts Commercial Messaging will expand from specific projects to enterprise-wide infrastructure role; users should choose products that can adapt to much larger role

metric_value: HP indicated transaction support within next 2 years (by approximately 1997)
notes: Based on HP stated roadmap; Aberdeen does not independently verify; HP OpenMail subsequently discontinued without achieving full Commercial Messaging status

metric_value: market leader in core Commercial Messaging; full-service bundled solution with deep training, consulting, and support
notes: IBM taking leadership role in core Commercial Messaging market; Aberdeen recommends MQSeries as primary product for enterprise IS buyers

metric_value: staged: (1) skeleton on directory nodes, (2) 2/3/4-tier LAN deployment, (3) WAN extension for targets of opportunity
notes: Aberdeen recommends three-stage deployment strategy: enterprise skeleton via directory/repository nodes, departmental LAN placement, then WAN extension

metric_value: Commercial Messaging + enterprise-wide directory/name service + enterprise-wide systems management
notes: Aberdeen defines the required enterprise infrastructure components; Commercial Messaging as keystone; directory and systems management as essential co-components

metric_value: renamed IBM MQ; remained active enterprise messaging platform for 30+ years
notes: Aberdeen's market leadership prediction for MQSeries fully verified; product renamed IBM MQ in 2014 and remains in active enterprise use

metric_value: CORBA became largely obsolete; never provided mainstream Commercial Messaging
notes: Aberdeen's 1995 prediction that CORBA would not provide Commercial Messaging verified; CORBA largely displaced by SOAP/REST/message queuing

metric_value: acquired by IBM for $3.5 billion in 1995
notes: IBM acquired Lotus in 1995 shortly after this study was published; Notes subsequently transferred to HCL Technologies in 2019

metric_value: verified; became dominant messaging paradigm (Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SNS/SQS, Azure Service Bus)
notes: Aberdeen's 1995 prediction of pub/sub proliferation fully confirmed by 2010s event-streaming ecosystem; market grew to multi-billion dollars

metric_value: verified; enterprise integration and API modernization debt became multi-billion dollar challenge
notes: Aberdeen's 1995 warning about growing legacy networking-limited application debt proved prescient; enterprise integration became a major market segment

metric_value: dissolved; sold assets approximately 2001
notes: Banyan Systems dissolved around 2001 after selling VINES and related assets; workflow toolset did not achieve sustained commercial success

metric_value: acquired by Sun Microsystems 1999; then Oracle 2010; Forte CADE discontinued

metric_value: acquired by Sybase 1995; then SAP 2010; PowerBuilder continues as Appeon PowerBuilder
notes: Powersoft was acquired by Sybase in 1995 (same year as study); Sybase then acquired by SAP; PowerBuilder continues under Appeon

metric_value: Aberdeen predicts IBM MQSeries will dominate enterprise commercial messaging; recommends immediate adoption as enterprise infrastructure
notes: Aberdeen explicitly recommends MQSeries as strategic infrastructure; prediction of market leadership embedded in recommendation text.

metric_name: IBM MQSeries market leadership — actual outcome
metric_value: IBM MQSeries did achieve dominant enterprise messaging position; renamed IBM MQ in 2014; still a top enterprise messaging platform in 2026
notes: Prediction confirmed: IBM MQ (formerly MQSeries) remains a primary enterprise messaging backbone. Aberdeen's recommendation proved correct.

metric_value: Aberdeen predicts that companies not adopting Commercial Messaging will accumulate a growing mass of legacy networking-limited applications requiring costly retrofits
notes: Aberdeen warning: failure to adopt = legacy debt. Explicit prediction about future architectural burden.

metric_value: Confirmed: enterprises that delayed messaging adoption faced costly SOA/ESB modernization projects in 2000s; message broker architectures became multi-billion dollar market (IBM WebSphere MQ, TIBCO, webMethods)
notes: Prediction confirmed: integration middleware became a massive market. ESB/SOA wave of 2000s was direct consequence of deferred messaging investment.
